title: Trailers
courseTitle: Learn the HTTP Protocol in Go
readme:
# Trailers

Did you think it was a bit odd that we had to write `0\r\n\r\n` (two registered nurses) to signal the end of the chunked encoding?

There's actually a good reason for it – you can have _additional headers_ at the end of chunked encoding, called (of course) [Trailers](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9112#section-7.1.2). They work the same way that headers do with one catch: **you have to specify the names of the trailers in a `Trailer` header**. For example:

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/plain
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Trailer: Lane, Prime, TJ

1E
I could go for a cup of coffee
C
But not Java
12
Never go full Java
0
Lane: goober
Prime: chill-guy
TJ: 1-indexer

```

They go _between_ the two `\r\n` sequences in our original chunked encoding example.

## Why Trailers?

Trailers are often used to send information about the message body that **can't be known until the message body is fully sent**. For example, the [hash](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptographic_hash_function) of the message body... in fact, let's do that.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add a new method to your `response` package that does what you'd expect based on your knowledge of trailers. I used `func (w *Writer) WriteTrailers(h headers.Headers) error`.

> [!tip]
> Remember, trailers are formatted just like headers.

2. [ ] In your proxy handler (the `httpbin.org` one):
   - [ ] Ensure that a request to `/httpbin/html` on your server will proxy `https://httpbin.org/html` instead of the streaming endpoint we used before. We'll be using that endpoint for this assignment.
   - [ ] Announce `X-Content-SHA256` and `X-Content-Length` as trailers in the `Trailer` header.
   - [ ] Remove the `Content-Length` header.
   - [ ] Keep track of the full response body as you read it in chunks from the `httpbin` server.
   - [ ] Once you're done writing the chunks, calculate the hash of the full response body using [`sha256.Sum256`](https://pkg.go.dev/crypto/sha256#Sum256).
   - [ ] Add the following headers:
     - `X-Content-SHA256: <hash>`
     - `X-Content-Length: <length of raw body in bytes>`
3. [ ] Run your server, and debug your code with `curl --raw`.

> [!info]
> HTTP header names are _case-insensitive_ by specification. Some servers and clients may canonicalize header names in certain ways (e.g., `X-Content-Sha256`), so don't be surprised if you notice discrepancies. It's best not to rely on any particular casing when working with headers.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests **with your server running**.

title: Binary Data
courseTitle: Learn the HTTP Protocol in Go
readme:
# Binary Data

As we've covered, HTTP is a text-based protocol, but **that doesn't mean it can't send binary data**. In fact, HTTP is quite good at sending binary data, and it does so by using the `Content-Type` header to indicate the type of data being sent. For example, if you're sending an image, you might use `Content-Type: image/png`, and if you're sending a video, you might use `Content-Type: video/mp4`.

That way, the client knows how to interpret the body. Rather than reading it as raw text, it can expect a specific format of video data, for example.

## Assignment

The beauty of our current abstraction is that we already have all the tools we need to respond with binary data! **Let's add a handler that responds with a video**.

1. [ ] Use curl to download this video file:

```
mkdir assets
curl -o assets/vim.mp4 https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/vim-vs-neovim-prime.mp4
```

> [!tip]
> Add the `assets` folder to `.gitignore`, so you don't accidentally commit it.

2. [ ] Add a new handler that responds to the `GET /video` endpoint with the video file. Use the `Content-Type: video/mp4` header. I used [`os.ReadFile`](https://pkg.go.dev/os#ReadFile) to just read the whole file into memory (probably not the most efficient, but works for our demo).
3. [ ] Navigate to `http://localhost:42069/video` in your browser... does it work?

**Pretty cool right!** This stuff's not so scary.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: HTTP 2 and 3
courseTitle: Learn the HTTP Protocol in Go
readme:
# HTTP 2 and 3

First of all, great job! You've made it through the TCP to HTTP course!

> [!prime]
> My goal with this course is to dispel magic. Not the magic of discovering something new, the enjoyment of a new technology or programming language, the ability to see a problem from a new angle. No, magic that makes you feel like you just might not be capable enough. You are capable.

That said, you've learned a whole lot about HTTP/1.1, but I'd be remiss if I didn't just leave you with a brief teaser of HTTP 2 and 3, because you will see them around, and they're very different from HTTP/1.1. However, it's notable that _a lot_ of the internet still runs on HTTP/1.1.

## [HTTP/2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP/2)

Some key differences include:

- It's a binary protocol rather than text-based. This makes it more efficient and less error-prone, but also requires more steps to debug, typically.
- HTTP/2 uses [multiplexing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiplexing), which allows multiple requests and responses to be sent over a single connection at the same time. This reduces latency and improves performance.
- Uses [header compression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HPACK) to save on header bandwidth.
- Allows for server push, which lets the server send resources to the client before they are requested.

## [HTTP/3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP/3)

- Built on [QUIC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QUIC) instead of TCP. QUIC is a transport layer protocol that runs over UDP, which allows for faster connection establishment and improved performance.
- Mandates encryption at the HTTP protocol level (HTTP/1.1 is unencrypted by default, meaning it requires [HTTPS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTPS) to be secure).
